Premium Matcha & Hojicha Sourcing for U.S. Cafés and Brands

Reliable access to premium matcha and hojicha for U.S. cafés and brands, with verified origin and long-term supply support.

Latest News
Demand
$500M+
Japanese green tea exports surpassed $500 million in 2025, reaching record highs. The United States is now the largest export market.
Supply
645
Tea-farming households remaining in Kyoto Prefecture, down from 1,679 in 2008, according to Kyoto Prefecture data. Succession challenges continue to reduce production capacity.
Built for factories, not cafés
Most wholesale matcha is made for food manufacturing, not for drinks. Sourcing guides rarely explain the difference.
Verification risk
No universal standard governs matcha grade classifications
Labels such as "ceremonial grade" remain inconsistent across international markets. Origin claims are often difficult to verify.

How we help

Better sourcing decisions before you place the order.

For cafés, hotels, restaurants, and beverage brands sourcing Japanese tea in the U.S., we help you verify origin, pick the right grade for each use, get the paperwork right, and plan supply so your menu keeps running when the market shifts.

01

Origin Verification

Understand what regional labels actually mean in Japan and how to verify origin claims before placing an order. The Uji designation legally covers four prefectures, a distinction many sourcing guides do not explain.

02

Grade by Use Case

Latte programs, straight-service matcha, pastry applications, and retail products each require different sourcing logic. Paying ceremonial-grade prices for a latte program is usually a budget choice, not a cup-quality upgrade.

03

Documentation

Certificates of Analysis, Organic JAS certification, FDA compliance, and EU pesticide MRL requirements. Understand what each document covers and what a capable supplier should provide without prompting.

04

Supply Continuity

As Kyoto tencha supply continues to tighten, backup sourcing strategies are becoming increasingly important for operators who depend on matcha.

Featured
Sourcing

Japan Filed for National GI Status on Japanese Tea: What It Means for Buyers

Japan's tea industry recently filed a national Geographical Indication application in response to concerns around counterfeit Uji matcha and international trademark disputes. If approved, Japanese tea would become Japan's second nationally protected GI product after sake. As green tea exports reached a record $500M+ in 2025, questions around origin verification and protected standards are increasingly relevant for U.S. buyers.

Read the full story

Latest from the Journal

All stories
Tea Craft

"Ceremonial Grade" Has No Legal Definition in Japan.

No government body in Japan formally defines ceremonial grade. Producers set their own thresholds. Measurable variables include shading duration, harvest timing, tencha processing methods, and milling practices.

Tea Craft · May 2025
Sourcing

How to Source Matcha for Your Café

Latte-grade and straight-service matcha require different sourcing decisions. Origin claims, grade matching, and documentation requirements vary significantly across suppliers.

Sourcing · April 2025
Cafés

The Boutique Matcha Bar Model That Chain Operators Are Watching

An increasing number of specialty cafés source directly from Japanese farms and provide documented origin information to guests. These sourcing decisions increasingly influence both pricing and menu development.

Cafés · March 2025
Supply

Kyoto Has Fewer Than 650 Tea Farmers Left. What Does It Mean for Your Supply Chain?

Kyoto had 1,679 tea-farming households in 2008. By 2023, that number had fallen to 645. More than half of producers are over sixty years old, and many do not have identified successors. Businesses without diversified sourcing strategies may face increasing supply risk in the seasons ahead.

Read more
Culture

Uji Is Not a Town. It Is a Regional Tea Designation.

The Kyoto Prefecture Tea Industry Council defines Uji matcha as tea finished by Kyoto-based businesses using leaves from Kyoto, Nara, Shiga, and Mie. Uji City itself has around 80 hectares of tea gardens. Buyers pay considerably more for Uji-labeled products. What the label covers and how to verify it for an import order are two different conversations.

Read more
Supply

Why Hojicha Is Becoming Harder to Find

Supply pressure is no longer limited to matcha. Hojicha, long considered an everyday tea in Japan, is also becoming harder to source as global demand rises. Growing international interest, declining tea production capacity, and shifts toward higher-value matcha production are changing the market. For cafés and brands, long-term supply planning is becoming increasingly important.

Read more

Tea Craft

The Seven Production Variables That Actually Determine Matcha Quality

Producer, cultivar, origin, harvest timing, shade method, drying process, and milling method each affect the final cup. None of these variables reliably appear on retail packaging.

Tea Craft · February 2025
Market

Is Matcha Going to Replace Coffee?

U.S. matcha retail sales rose 77% over the past three years, while coffee remains the dominant daily habit. Growth is emerging in afternoon drinks, wellness-focused purchases, and premium café menus.

Read more
Market · May 2026
Sourcing

What Fake Reseller Risk Actually Looks Like Before You Place an Order

Common indicators include origin claims that cannot be traced to a specific producer, COAs issued by a broker rather than the processing facility, and pricing that does not align with the stated grade specification.

Sourcing · January 2025
On positioning

The U.S. has a growing number of matcha brands. The verified-origin, transparent-sourcing tier is still relatively thin.

MATCHA MÉTIER focuses on the part most buyers still find difficult: verified origin, grade selection by use case, documentation, and supply continuity.
